title: SEGMENT 12 — Immune-related adverse events: the dark side of brake-cutting

lines.text: SEGMENT 12 — Immune-related adverse events: the dark side of brake-cutting
Now let me get serious about toxicity, because we have skimmed it and it deserves its own segment.
When you remove the brakes on T cells systemically, the T cells you unleash do not only attack the tumor.
They attack, in some patients, more or less anything.
The immune-related adverse events, the irAEs, can in principle hit any organ.
They are protean — meaning they take many shapes, change between patients, surprise even experienced clinicians.
They are, as a class, the dark side of checkpoint inhibition.
Let me give you the catalog, organ by organ.
Skin.
Most common.
Rashes, pruritus, vitiligo, sometimes severe forms like Stevens-Johnson syndrome.

Vitiligo, where T cells attack melanocytes and destroy pigment, is interesting because in melanoma patients it correlates with response — the T cells that are killing the cancer's melanocytes are also killing the patient's healthy ones.
Some patients develop blotchy white patches across their face and trunk that are, in their own way, a sign that the drug is working.
Colon.
Colitis.
Diarrhea, abdominal pain, bloody stools.
In severe cases, perforation.
This is one of the most common reasons patients are hospitalized on these drugs.
Treatment is high-dose steroids, sometimes infliximab — yes, the same anti-TNF antibody used for inflammatory bowel disease — and sometimes permanent discontinuation of the immunotherapy.
Liver.
Hepatitis.
Elevated liver enzymes, sometimes massive, requiring drug discontinuation and steroids.
Most cases resolve, but some leave permanent injury.
Lungs.
Pneumonitis.
Inflammation of lung tissue.
Cough, shortness of breath, hypoxia.
CT scans show ground-glass infiltrates.
Severe pneumonitis can be fatal, particularly in patients with underlying lung disease, which is a lot of lung-cancer patients.
Pneumonitis is one of the most feared toxicities in lung cancer immunotherapy.
Endocrine system.
Thyroiditis — leading often to permanent hypothyroidism.
Hypophysitis — inflammation of the pituitary gland, sometimes causing permanent hormonal deficiencies.
Adrenalitis — Type 1 diabetes, occasionally, particularly with combination therapy.
The endocrinopathies are notable because they are often permanent.
A patient who develops checkpoint-inhibitor-induced hypothyroidism takes thyroid replacement for the rest of their life.
The brake was cut, the gland was destroyed, the gland does not come back.
Heart.
Myocarditis.
Rare — perhaps one to two percent of patients — but often catastrophic.
T cells infiltrate the heart muscle, the heart fails, the patient dies.
Mortality from checkpoint-inhibitor myocarditis, when it happens, is around fifty percent.
It is the single most-feared rare toxicity.
Nervous system.
Encephalitis, myasthenia gravis, Guillain-Barre-like syndromes, peripheral neuropathies.
Rare but serious.
Joints, muscles, kidneys, eyes, blood cells.
The list goes on.
Pretty much any organ that has a tissue antigen that can be presented to T cells can become a target.
A few important features of irAEs.
First, they can occur late.
Most adverse drug reactions you learn about in pharmacology have a clear temporal relationship with the drug.
You take the drug, the side effect happens, you stop the drug, the side effect resolves.
Not so with checkpoint inhibitors.
irAEs can develop months after the drug has been stopped.
The immune memory persists.
A patient who stopped pembrolizumab two years ago can still present, this week, with pembrolizumab-induced colitis.
The drug is gone but the unleashed T cells remain.
Second, the management requires immunosuppression.

Severe irAEs are treated with high-dose corticosteroids, often for weeks or months, and refractory cases are escalated to other immunosuppressants — infliximab, mycophenolate, sometimes more aggressive agents.
There is a long-running concern that immunosuppression might also dampen the anti-tumor response.
The data so far suggest that short-course steroids don't significantly reduce efficacy, but the question is real.
Third, irAEs may correlate with efficacy.
Patients who develop irAEs tend, on average, to do better against their cancer than patients who don't.
The same immune activation that's destroying the cancer is also destroying healthy tissue; if you don't see the collateral damage, maybe you're also not seeing the tumor damage.
This is a population-level observation; individual patients vary enormously.
Fourth, patients with pre-existing autoimmunity present a special problem.
People with rheumatoid arthritis, lupus, ulcerative colitis, multiple sclerosis — they have, in some sense, T cells already inclined to attack their own tissues.
Giving them a checkpoint inhibitor risks catastrophic flares.
These patients are often excluded from clinical trials, and in clinical practice they are treated cautiously, if at all.
We do not yet have a great answer for how to extend the benefits of immunotherapy to patients with autoimmune comorbidities.
The bottom line.
Checkpoint inhibitors are powerful drugs, and the power comes with a price.
The price is, in essence, the cost of cutting brakes that were installed for a reason.
Most patients tolerate the drugs well.
A meaningful minority do not.
A small but real number die of the toxicity.
Managing irAEs has become a clinical subspecialty of its own — there are now consensus guidelines, dedicated immunotoxicity clinics, specialized teams at every comprehensive cancer center.
This is the dark side of the revolution.
It is real.
It is manageable in most cases.
It is occasionally devastating.
And it is the price we pay for the durable remissions on the other side of the ledger.
